chunks.text: Tactile Sensing and Teletaction Research Teletaction System Current force-reflecting teleoperators provide information on the net force of contact, but do not provide information about static texture, local compliance, or local shape. For dextrous m

local compliance, or local shape. For dextrous manipulation, tactile (i.e. cutaneous) feedback should be provided to the operator. A tactile sensor array can be used to sense contact properties remotely. To provide local shape information, an arr

motely. To provide local shape information, an array of force generators can create a pressure distribution on a finger tip, synthesizing an approximation to a true contact. To implement tactile feedback, we are considering the problems of tactile t

back, we are considering the problems of tactile transduction, signal processing, tactile display, and human perception. We have developed a prototype 5 by 5 pressure display with 3 bits of resolution and are developing a 1 mm square tactile sensor

n and are developing a 1 mm square tactile sensor with 8 by 8 elements. One of our long-term goals is to fabricate a tactile sensing catheter which will allow surgeons to remotely feel tissue properties during minimally invasive surgery. Pneumatic Ta

es during minimally invasive surgery. Pneumatic Tactile Displays The prototype 5x5 pneumatic display (on the left) designed by Michael Cohn has a max force range of 0.3 N per element, 3 dB point of 8 Hz, and 3 bits of force resolution. It uses indivi

Hz, and 3 bits of force resolution. It uses individually machined pins, with 5 atm pressure. Our current tactile display (on the right) is one piece, and molded from silicone rubber. Advantages are ease of fabrication, no air leakage, and no pin fri

ase of fabrication, no air leakage, and no pin friction. ( A Compliant Tactile Display for Teletaction, ) < pdf > As seen below, we can also mold curved compliant tactile displays. See Gabe Moy's PhD thesis Bidigital Teletaction System Design and P

D thesis Bidigital Teletaction System Design and Performance CAD drawings and construction details for compliant pneumatic tactile display design Human Tactile Perception The limitations of human tactile perception need to be understood when designi

tile perception need to be understood when designing a teletaction system. We model an ideal teletaction system simply using two elastic layers placed between the finger and an object. The tactile sensor layer transmits contact stress to a display l

nsor layer transmits contact stress to a display layer. This system has no spatial sampling limits or level quantization, and allows us to study ideal information transmission, considering the effects of spatial anti-aliasing and reconstruction filte

of spatial anti-aliasing and reconstruction filters in the sensor and display respectively. One of the important design issues is whether the tactile display needs to display shear as well as normal stress information. While the utility of shear str

stress information. While the utility of shear stress display is application dependent, for applications such as surgery, where tissue is quite slippery, we have shown in a series of experiments that finer details are resolved when shear stress is no

finer details are resolved when shear stress is not transmitted. (For more details, see [Moy and Fearing 1998].) Psychophysics results for grating resolution are shown below: (Averages of data from 10 subjects. Each point represents the results of 30

subjects. Each point represents the results of 300 trials testing identification of horizontal and vertical gratings.) Papers Note: postscript .ps files can be displayed/printed using ghostview . Postcript is generally printer independent unlike PDF
